purpose: A one-hour stakeholder exercise to define preliminary requirements for a fictional autonomous robot.
disclaimer: This workshop is designed to generate stakeholder intelligence that may inform a Product Requirements Document.

robot.name: Autonomous Hydrovac
robot.task: The robot receives a list of reported utility locations and verifies each one — it travels to the location, confirms its position, establishes a safe work area, uses pressurized water and vacuum excavation to expose the utility, documents what it finds, reports the verified location, and moves to its next assignment.
robot.environment: An active construction site with buried electrical, gas, water, data, and communication utilities. Utility records or surface markings have already been provided.
robot.autonomyLevel: Autonomous operation with human intervention and teleoperation available when it encounters unknown or unsafe conditions.
robot.consumables: Onboard water for excavation, onboard debris/spoils storage, and fuel or battery capacity.
process: Uses water and vacuum excavation to expose the utility
assumptions: The robot works on an active construction site
Utility records or surface markings have already been provided
The robot receives a list of locations to investigate
It carries water and stores excavated material onboard
It must document the utility before leaving
A person can intervene when it encounters an unknown condition
unresolvedQuestions: How will people interact with it?
How should it operate and fail safely?

sections.title: Users & Robot Capabilities
sections.objective: Define what the robot must do and who will use it.
sections.mainPrompt: What must this robot accomplish, for whom, and under what jobsite conditions?
sections.scenario: The robot has been assigned several reported utility locations on an active construction site. At each location it must carefully expose the utility and document its actual location, depth, direction, apparent type, and visible condition. It may use cameras, positioning systems, utility-detection sensors, a robotic boom, pressurized water, a vacuum excavation system, and remote monitoring or teleoperation.
discuss: Who is the primary user?
What must the robot be able to do?
What decisions can it make autonomously?
What decisions still require human approval?
What training should an operator need?
What conditions should cause the robot to stop?
What information must it provide after completing the work?

sections.title: Workflow & Jobsite Deployment
sections.objective: Define how the robot fits into the complete construction workflow.
sections.mainPrompt: What must happen before, during, and after the robot performs its work?
sections.scenario: The robot receives a mission with several utility locations that must be verified before construction can continue. At each it must travel to the work area, confirm the location, check surrounding conditions, establish a safe work zone, position equipment, expose and document the utility, protect or close the opening, report the result, and move on. Mid-mission it discovers: a location blocked by stored materials, a location with poor connectivity, a utility not found at the expected depth, an unexpected exposed utility, a full debris tank, and another crew waiting on results.
discuss: How is a mission created and approved?
What must be ready before the robot arrives?
How should it move through an active site?
What can it do when connectivity is lost?
When should teleoperation be required?
How are water and excavated material managed?
What happens when the utility cannot be found?
How are results delivered to downstream crews?

sections.title: Safety, Failure & Human Interaction
sections.objective: Define how the robot should operate, interact, stop, and recover safely.
sections.mainPrompt: How should the system prevent harm, stop safely, and recover when assumptions fail?
sections.scenario: The robot combines several hazards: a large autonomous vehicle, a moving robotic boom, pressurized water, powerful vacuum equipment, open excavations, nearby workers and equipment, and live electrical, gas, water, data, and communication utilities. A successful failure doesn't mean nothing went wrong — it means the robot recognizes it cannot safely continue, stops in a predictable condition, protects workers and the utility, communicates what happened, prevents an uncontrolled restart, and waits for an authorized person to recover it.
discuss: What are the three greatest hazards?
Who has the right of way?
Who can stop the robot?
What happens when communication is lost?
What happens when a critical sensor fails?
How should the robot respond to an unknown utility?
What requires human inspection before restart?
What company procedures must be built into its workflow?

fields.label: Your definition of a successful failure
One task the robot must never perform autonomously
